outline.title: Calculus II
outline.description: A rigorous second course in calculus focused on techniques and theory for integration, infinite series, and selected applications of advanced calculus. The course emphasizes deriving rules from first principles where possible, then applying them in structured problem-solving with worked examples and precise reasoning.
learningObjectives: Evaluate definite and indefinite integrals using substitution, integration by parts, trigonometric identities, and partial fractions.
Use the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus to connect accumulation functions, antiderivatives, and definite integrals.
Compute areas, volumes, and arc lengths arising from planar regions and parametric or polar representations.
Analyze sequences and series for convergence using comparison, ratio, root, alternating, and integral tests.
Construct and use power series and Taylor polynomials to approximate functions and quantify error.
Work fluently with parametric equations, polar coordinates, and introductory differential equations in standard calculus settings.
prerequisites: Algebraic manipulation with fractions, exponents, radicals, and logarithms.
Trigonometric identities and equations at the level of precalculus or Calculus I.
Limits, continuity, and the meaning of derivative as a rate of change and slope.
Basic differentiation rules, including product, quotient, and chain rules.
Basic definite and indefinite integration, including the ability to evaluate simple antiderivatives.
Comfort reading and writing functions, graphs, and interval notation.

chapters.title: Fundamentals of Integration and the Fundamental Theorem
chapters.description: Rebuild the bridge from antiderivatives to accumulation. The learner derives the central theorem of integral calculus and uses it to evaluate definite integrals efficiently and correctly.
learningGoals: Explain why definite integrals measure accumulation and signed area.
State and apply both parts of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
Evaluate definite integrals by finding antiderivatives.
Interpret accumulation functions and differentiate them when the upper limit varies.
Recognize when a problem requires a geometric or analytical approach.
topics: Riemann sums and the definition of the definite integral
Worked examples with variable upper limits

chapters.title: Techniques of Integration I: Substitution and Algebraic Forms
chapters.description: Develop substitution as the inverse of the chain rule, then use it to simplify integrals involving composite functions and rational expressions.
learningGoals: Recognize when a substitution matches an inner derivative.
Choose substitutions that reduce an integral to a standard form.
Integrate algebraic and exponential expressions after substitution.
Check an antiderivative by differentiating it back to the integrand.
Set up and evaluate substitution-based definite integrals carefully under change of variables.
topics: Change of variables in definite integrals
Integrals involving powers of linear functions
Worked examples and verification by differentiation

chapters.title: Techniques of Integration II: Integration by Parts and Trigonometric Integrals
chapters.description: Use the product rule in reverse to handle products of functions, then manage trigonometric powers and products with identities and systematic reduction strategies.
learningGoals: Derive the integration by parts formula from the product rule.
Select effective choices for $u$ and $dv$ in common problems.
Evaluate integrals involving products of polynomials, exponentials, logarithms, and trig functions.
Reduce trigonometric integrals using identities and parity arguments.
Handle repeated integration by parts and recognize cyclic integrals.
topics: Integrals of $x^n e^x$, $x^n\sin x$, and $x^n\ln x$
Products of sine and cosine with different frequencies

chapters.title: Trigonometric Substitution and Partial Fractions
chapters.description: Learn two major methods for integrals that resist direct substitution: trig substitution for radical expressions and partial fractions for rational functions.
learningGoals: Identify radicals that suggest trigonometric substitution.
Transform radical integrals into trigonometric forms and simplify them.
Decompose rational functions into partial fractions correctly.
Integrate rational expressions with repeated and irreducible quadratic factors.
Translate back to the original variable and simplify results.
topics: Long division for improper rational functions
Distinct linear factors in partial fractions
Repeated linear factors and irreducible quadratics

chapters.title: Applications of Integration: Area, Volume, and Arc Length
chapters.description: Use definite integrals to compute geometric quantities derived from curves and regions. The emphasis is on setting up the integral correctly before evaluating it.
learningGoals: Translate geometric descriptions into definite integrals.
Compute areas between curves and with respect to either variable when appropriate.
Set up and evaluate volumes using disks, washers, and shells.
Derive and apply the arc length formula in standard cases.
Recognize when symmetry or geometry can shorten a computation.
topics: Area under a curve and between curves

chapters.title: Sequences and the Notion of Convergence
chapters.description: Introduce sequences as discrete limits and establish the language needed for infinite series. The chapter emphasizes rigorous limit reasoning and common convergence patterns.
learningGoals: Determine whether a sequence converges and compute its limit when possible.
Use monotonicity and boundedness to support convergence arguments.
Analyze recursively defined or formula-defined sequences.
Distinguish the behavior of sequences from the behavior of their terms in a series context.
Apply standard limit laws to sequences.

chapters.title: Infinite Series and Convergence Tests
chapters.description: Build a systematic toolkit for deciding whether an infinite series converges. The chapter proceeds from necessary conditions to comparison, ratio, root, and alternating methods.
learningGoals: Explain what it means for a series to converge.
Apply necessary divergence tests before using more advanced tests.
Use comparison and limit comparison to classify positive-term series.
Use ratio and root tests appropriately for factorials, exponentials, and powers.
Apply the alternating series test and distinguish conditional from absolute convergence.
topics: Direct comparison and limit comparison tests
Alternating series test and absolute versus conditional convergence

chapters.title: Power Series and Taylor Approximations
chapters.description: Extend the notion of series to function representation. Learn to generate power series, manipulate them formally within their radius of convergence, and use Taylor polynomials for approximation.
learningGoals: Recognize and construct power series representations of functions.
Determine radius and interval of convergence for common series.
Differentiate and integrate power series term by term within the interval of convergence.
Form Taylor and Maclaurin polynomials for standard functions.
Estimate approximation error using remainder ideas in practical settings.
topics: Geometric series as a generating template
Common expansions for $e^x$, $\sin x$, $\cos x$, and $\ln(1+x)$

chapters.title: Parametric and Polar Calculus; Introductory Differential Equations
chapters.description: Conclude with selected topics that use the full calculus toolkit on non-Cartesian curves and simple dynamic models. The learner differentiates and integrates in parametric and polar form, then solves basic separable differential equations.
learningGoals: Differentiate and integrate curves given parametrically.
Compute slopes, tangents, and area for polar curves.
Set up arc length and area formulas in parametric or polar coordinates.
Solve basic separable differential equations by direct integration.
Interpret simple differential equations as models of growth or decay.
topics: Parametric equations and elimination of the parameter
Arc length and area for parametric curves

weeklySchedule.focus: Core integration theory and first techniques
activities: Read each derivation carefully and rewrite the key formulas from memory.
Work several definite-integral problems using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
Practice identifying when $u$-substitution is appropriate before computing any integral.
Solve mixed integration sets that force method selection, not just execution.
Derive integration by parts from the product rule and verify answers by differentiation.
Set up area, volume, and arc-length problems before evaluating the integrals.
Classify sequences and series using a decision tree of convergence tests.
Explain in words why a chosen test applies before computing.
Build a comparison table of geometric, $p$-series, ratio, root, and alternating examples.

weeklySchedule.focus: Power series and selected advanced topics
activities: Expand standard functions into Taylor series and determine convergence intervals.
Use a few terms of a series to approximate a value and estimate the error.
Complete parametric, polar, and separable-differential-equation exercises as capstone practice.

milestones.title: Integral fluency checkpoint
milestones.description: Demonstrate correct use of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and $u$-substitution on standard problems.
milestones.deliverable: A solved set of definite and indefinite integrals with brief justification for each method choice.

milestones.title: Advanced integration checkpoint
milestones.description: Handle nontrivial integrals using integration by parts, trig identities, trig substitution, and partial fractions.
milestones.deliverable: A mixed-method problem set with fully worked solutions and method-selection notes.

milestones.title: Convergence checkpoint
milestones.description: Correctly classify a representative sample of sequences and series using appropriate convergence tests.
milestones.deliverable: A convergence analysis sheet showing test choice, execution, and conclusion for each item.

milestones.title: Series and applications capstone
milestones.description: Use power series, parametric/polar formulas, and separable equations in a coordinated problem set.
milestones.deliverable: A capstone submission containing a Taylor approximation, a polar/parametric computation, and a solved differential equation.

gamePlan.assessmentStrategy: Mastery is demonstrated by accurately solving unfamiliar problems while justifying each method choice and verifying results by differentiation, limit checks, or direct substitution. The learner should be able to explain why a convergence test applies, not merely state the answer.

chapterData.title: Fundamentals of Integration and the Fundamental Theorem
chapterData.description: Rebuild the bridge from antiderivatives to accumulation. The learner derives the central theorem of integral calculus and uses it to evaluate definite integrals efficiently and correctly.
learningGoals: Explain why definite integrals measure accumulation and signed area.
State and apply both parts of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
Evaluate definite integrals by finding antiderivatives.
Interpret accumulation functions and differentiate them when the upper limit varies.
Recognize when a problem requires a geometric or analytical approach.
topics: Riemann sums and the definition of the definite integral
Worked examples with variable upper limits

content.introduction:
Integration is the calculus counterpart to accumulation. If differentiation tells us how a quantity changes at an instant, integration tells us how much has been gathered over an interval: distance from velocity, mass from density, area from height, charge from current, probability from density. The central idea of this chapter is that the definite integral is not introduced as a mysterious symbol, but as the limit of increasingly accurate sums.

This chapter rebuilds the bridge between sums, area, and antiderivatives. First we define the definite integral through Riemann sums, so that the meaning of $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ is grounded in approximation. Then we develop the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, which explains why antiderivatives compute definite integrals efficiently and why accumulation functions can be differentiated when the upper limit varies. By the end, you should be able to decide whether a problem is best approached geometrically, by summation, or by antiderivatives, and to carry out each method carefully.

sections.title: 1. Riemann Sums and the Meaning of the Definite Integral
sections.content:
To understand the definite integral, start with the simplest question: how do we measure the total accumulation of a quantity whose rate is known only through a graph or formula? Suppose $f(x)$ gives a rate, such as velocity or height above the $x$-axis. Over an interval $[a,b]$, we divide the interval into small subintervals, estimate the contribution on each piece by a rectangle, and add the pieces. That rectangle-sum is the basic idea behind the definite integral.

Formally, choose a partition $a=x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_n=b$. Let $\Delta x_i=x_i-x_{i-1}$ and choose a sample point $x_i^*$ in each subinterval. The Riemann sum is $$\sum_{i=1}^n f(x_i^*)\,\Delta x_i.$$ If these sums approach a single number as the partition is refined, that limit is the definite integral: $$\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = \lim_{\|P\|\to 0} \sum_{i=1}^n f(x_i^*)\,\Delta x_i.$$ Here $\|P\|$ denotes the mesh size, the length of the widest subinterval. The intuition is that smaller rectangles produce a better approximation to the true accumulated amount.

Worked example: approximate $\int_0^2 x^2\,dx$ using right-endpoint Riemann sums with $n=4$ equal subintervals. We proceed step by step.
$$\Delta x = \frac{2-0}{4} = \frac12 \quad\text{(width of each subinterval)}$$
The right endpoints are
$$x_1^*=\frac12,\; x_2^*=1,\; x_3^*=\frac32,\; x_4^*=2 \quad\text{(sample points)}$$
Evaluate the function at those points:
$$f(x)=x^2 \Rightarrow f\left(\frac12\right)=\frac14,\; f(1)=1,\; f\left(\frac32\right)=\frac94,\; f(2)=4$$
Form the sum:
$$\sum_{i=1}^4 f(x_i^*)\Delta x = \left(\frac14+1+\frac94+4\right)\frac12 \quad\text{(rectangle areas)}$$
Combine the terms:
$$\left(\frac14+1+\frac94+4\right)=\frac{1+4+9+16}{4}=\frac{30}{4}=\frac{15}{2}$$
Multiply by $\frac12$:
$$\frac{15}{2}\cdot\frac12=\frac{15}{4}$$
So the right Riemann sum is $\frac{15}{4}$, an approximation to the true integral. We do not yet need the exact value to see the method: the integral is what the sum converges to as the rectangles get thinner.

This definition also explains signed area. If $f(x)\ge 0$ on $[a,b]$, then the integral measures ordinary geometric area under the curve. If $f(x)$ becomes negative, rectangles below the $x$-axis contribute negative area. So the definite integral tracks net accumulation, not just visible area. This distinction is essential later, because it tells us when to expect a positive result, a negative result, or cancellation.
examples: $\int_0^1 3\,dx$ as the accumulation of a constant rate
Right-endpoint Riemann sum for $\int_1^3 x\,dx$ with $n=2$
keyPoints: A definite integral is the limit of Riemann sums.
The definite integral measures net accumulation, not just geometric area.
The sign of $f(x)$ matters: values below the $x$-axis contribute negatively.
A finer partition usually gives a better approximation to the integral.

sections.title: 2. Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals
sections.content:
The next bridge is from accumulation to reversing differentiation. An antiderivative of a function $f$ is a function $F$ such that $F'(x)=f(x)$ on the relevant interval. In symbols, if $F'(x)=f(x)$, then $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$. The family of all antiderivatives differs only by a constant, because derivatives erase constant shifts.

The indefinite integral notation summarizes this family: $$\int f(x)\,dx = F(x)+C,$$ where $C$ is an arbitrary constant. This notation is not yet a definite integral; it represents all antiderivatives of $f$. It is useful because the Fundamental Theorem will later connect antiderivatives directly to definite integrals.

Worked example: find an antiderivative of $f(x)=4x^3-2x+5$. We do this term by term, using the rule that differentiation of powers lowers the exponent by $1$.
$$\int (4x^3-2x+5)\,dx = \int 4x^3\,dx - \int 2x\,dx + \int 5\,dx \quad\text{(linearity)}$$
Integrate each term:
$$\int 4x^3\,dx = x^4 \quad\text{since } \frac{d}{dx}(x^4)=4x^3$$
$$\int 2x\,dx = x^2 \quad\text{since } \frac{d}{dx}(x^2)=2x$$
$$\int 5\,dx = 5x \quad\text{since } \frac{d}{dx}(5x)=5$$
Combine the results and include the constant:
$$\int (4x^3-2x+5)\,dx = x^4-x^2+5x+C$$
Check by differentiating:
$$\frac{d}{dx}(x^4-x^2+5x+C)=4x^3-2x+5$$
So the antiderivative is correct.

This example shows the practical meaning of antiderivatives: they are reverse derivatives, and they are usually found by recognizing derivative patterns. The constant $C$ is not optional decoration; it records the fact that infinitely many functions have the same derivative. In definite integrals, that constant will disappear, which is exactly why evaluating accumulation over an interval becomes so efficient.
keyPoints: An antiderivative $F$ satisfies $F'(x)=f(x)$.
The indefinite integral $\int f(x)\,dx$ denotes the full family of antiderivatives.
The constant of integration $C$ represents the fact that antiderivatives differ by constants.
Checking an antiderivative is done by differentiating it.

sections.title: 3. The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 1
sections.content:
Part 1 of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus explains why accumulation functions are differentiable. Suppose $f$ is continuous on an interval and define an accumulation function by $$A(x)=\int_a^x f(t)\,dt.$$ This function measures the net accumulated amount from the fixed starting point $a$ to a variable endpoint $x$. The theorem says that, under continuity, $$A'(x)=f(x).$$ In words: the derivative of the accumulation function is the original rate function.

The intuition comes from a small change in the upper limit. If $x$ changes to $x+h$, then the new accumulated amount differs by the tiny slice $$A(x+h)-A(x)=\int_x^{x+h} f(t)\,dt.$$ If $h$ is small and $f$ is continuous, then over that short interval the function is nearly constant, so the area is approximately $f(x)h$. Dividing by $h$ suggests that the difference quotient approaches $f(x)$.

Worked example: let $$A(x)=\int_0^x (t^2+1)\,dt.$$ Find $A'(x)$. We use the theorem directly, but we also verify it by evaluating the integral first.
By Part 1,
$$A'(x)=x^2+1 \quad\text{(derivative of an accumulation function)}$$
To confirm, compute $A(x)$ explicitly:
$$A(x)=\int_0^x (t^2+1)\,dt = \left[\frac{t^3}{3}+t\right]_0^x \quad\text{(antiderivative)}$$
Evaluate at the bounds:
$$A(x)=\left(\frac{x^3}{3}+x\right)-\left(0+0\right)=\frac{x^3}{3}+x$$
Differentiate this result:
$$A'(x)=x^2+1$$
The two methods agree.

Part 1 is especially powerful when the upper limit is variable and the lower limit is fixed. It tells us that integration and differentiation are inverse processes, at least in the sense that taking the derivative of the accumulation returns the original integrand. That is the conceptual heart of why integrals can be computed by antiderivatives at all.
examples: Accumulation from a fixed start point to a moving endpoint
keyPoints: If $A(x)=\int_a^x f(t)\,dt$ and $f$ is continuous, then $A'(x)=f(x)$.
The derivative of an accumulation function recovers the integrand.
A small increase in the upper limit adds a thin slice of area.
Part 1 is the analytic version of “rate in, accumulation out.”

sections.title: 4. The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 2
sections.content:
Part 2 turns Part 1 into a computation tool. If $F$ is any antiderivative of a continuous function $f$ on $[a,b]$, then $$\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = F(b)-F(a).$$ This is the main practical formula for definite integrals. It says that rather than summing infinitely many thin rectangles directly, we can evaluate a single antiderivative at the endpoints and subtract.

Why is this true? Define $A(x)=\int_a^x f(t)\,dt$. By Part 1, $A'(x)=f(x)$, so $A$ is an antiderivative of $f$. Therefore $A(x)=F(x)+C$ for some constant $C$. Now evaluate at $x=a$ and $x=b$. Since $A(a)=\int_a^a f(t)\,dt=0$, the constant is determined so that the difference $A(b)-A(a)$ equals $F(b)-F(a)$. Thus the net accumulation depends only on endpoint values of an antiderivative.

Worked example: evaluate $$\int_1^3 (2x+4)\,dx.$$ We proceed step by step.
Find an antiderivative:
$$\int (2x+4)\,dx = x^2+4x+C \quad\text{(integrate term by term)}$$
Apply Part 2:
$$\int_1^3 (2x+4)\,dx = \left[x^2+4x\right]_1^3 \quad\text{(endpoint evaluation)}$$
Evaluate at the upper limit:
$$3^2+4\cdot 3=9+12=21$$
Evaluate at the lower limit:
$$1^2+4\cdot 1=1+4=5$$
Subtract:
$$21-5=16$$
So $$\int_1^3 (2x+4)\,dx=16.$$ 

Part 2 is valid because the integrand is continuous here, and continuity is the standard hypothesis that guarantees the theorem applies. In practice, this means that most functions encountered in an introductory calculus course can be integrated by antiderivatives once we know how to find them. The theorem also clarifies why the constant of integration is unnecessary for definite integrals: any constant added to $F$ cancels in $F(b)-F(a)$.
examples: Endpoint subtraction after finding an antiderivative
keyPoints: If $F'(x)=f(x)$, then $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = F(b)-F(a)$.
Definite integrals can be computed by evaluating an antiderivative at the bounds.
The constant of integration cancels in definite integrals.
Continuity of $f$ is the standard condition ensuring the theorem applies.

sections.title: 5. Basic Definite Integrals and Variable Upper Limits
sections.content:
Now we combine the two parts of the theorem with standard antiderivatives to evaluate basic definite integrals and to differentiate accumulation functions with moving endpoints. The strategy is simple but precise: identify the integrand, find an antiderivative, and subtract endpoint values. If the upper limit is variable, use Part 1 to differentiate the accumulation function directly.

Worked example 1: evaluate $$\int_0^\pi \sin x\,dx.$$ First find an antiderivative:
$$\int \sin x\,dx = -\cos x + C \quad\text{(since } \frac{d}{dx}(-\cos x)=\sin x\text{)}$$
Apply Part 2:
$$\int_0^\pi \sin x\,dx = \left[-\cos x\right]_0^\pi$$
Evaluate the endpoints:
$$-\cos(\pi)-\big(-\cos(0)\big)= -(-1)-(-1)=1+1=2$$
So the integral equals $2$. Geometrically, this is the area under $\sin x$ on $[0,\pi]$, and because $\sin x\ge 0$ there, the net area is positive.

Worked example 2: differentiate an accumulation function with a variable upper limit. Let $$B(x)=\int_2^x \frac{1}{1+t^2}\,dt.$$ Find $B'(x)$. By Part 1,
$$B'(x)=\frac{1}{1+x^2}.$$ 
To see this in context, notice that $B(x)$ measures accumulated growth from $2$ to $x$. Changing the endpoint slightly adds a thin slice of area whose thickness is the change in $x$ and whose height is approximately the value of the integrand at the new endpoint.

Worked example 3: recognize when geometry is easier than antiderivatives. Evaluate $$\int_{-2}^2 (4-|x|)\,dx.$$ Here the graph is a V-shaped tent with peak $4$ at $x=0$ and zeros at $x=\pm 4$, so on $[-2,2]$ the graph is entirely above the axis. Since the function is even, the integral can be split symmetrically:
$$\int_{-2}^2 (4-|x|)\,dx = 2\int_0^2 (4-x)\,dx \quad\text{(symmetry)}$$
Find an antiderivative of $4-x$:
$$\int (4-x)\,dx = 4x-\frac{x^2}{2}+C$$
Evaluate on $[0,2]$:
$$\int_0^2 (4-x)\,dx = \left[4x-\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^2 = \left(8-2\right)-0=6$$
Multiply by $2$:
$$2\cdot 6=12$$
So the integral equals $12$. This example shows that a geometric or symmetry-based approach can be more efficient than direct symbolic manipulation.

The central decision in practice is not merely “can I integrate?” but “what is the best interpretation of the problem?” If the graph is simple, geometry may be fastest. If an antiderivative is easy, Part 2 is usually best. If the problem asks about how an accumulated quantity changes with its endpoint, Part 1 is the right tool.
keyPoints: Use Part 2 to compute definite integrals from antiderivatives.
Use Part 1 to differentiate accumulation functions with variable upper limits.
Symmetry and geometry can simplify some integrals more than algebra can.
Always ask whether the problem is about area, accumulation, or changing accumulation.

exercises.question: Which statement best explains what the definite integral $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ measures?
exercises.solution: The definite integral measures net accumulation, or signed area, over the interval $[a,b]$. Values of $f(x)$ above the $x$-axis contribute positively, while values below the $x$-axis contribute negatively. So it is not just geometric area; it is total signed accumulation.

exercises.question: Use the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus to evaluate $\int_0^3 (2x+1)\,dx$. Show your steps.
exercises.solution:
Find an antiderivative of $2x+1$. One is $F(x)=x^2+x$ because $F'(x)=2x+1$.

Now apply Part 2 of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus:
$$\int_0^3 (2x+1)\,dx = F(3)-F(0).$$
Compute the endpoint values:
$$F(3)=3^2+3=12, \quad F(0)=0^2+0=0.$$ 
Therefore,
$$\int_0^3 (2x+1)\,dx=12-0=12.$$

exercises.question: Let $A(x)=\int_1^x (t^2-4)\,dt$. Find $A'(x)$ and explain why.
exercises.solution:
Since the integrand $t^2-4$ is continuous, Part 1 of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus applies.

If
$$A(x)=\int_1^x (t^2-4)\,dt,$$
then differentiating the accumulation function with respect to its upper limit gives
$$A'(x)=x^2-4.$$

This works because a small increase in $x$ adds a thin slice of area whose rate of change is the integrand evaluated at the moving upper limit.

exercises.question: Suppose $F'(x)=f(x)$ and $\int_2^5 f(x)\,dx=7$. What is $F(5)-F(2)$?
exercises.solution:
By Part 2 of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus,
$$\int_2^5 f(x)\,dx = F(5)-F(2).$$
Since the integral equals $7$, it follows that
$$F(5)-F(2)=7.$$

exercises.question: A student says, "A definite integral is just the area under a curve." Explain why that statement is sometimes correct and sometimes misleading.
exercises.solution: The statement is only partly correct. A definite integral does measure area when the function stays nonnegative on the interval, because all contributions lie above the $x$-axis. But in general a definite integral measures signed accumulation. If the graph goes below the $x$-axis, those parts contribute negatively and reduce the total. So the integral is better understood as net accumulation rather than always being literal geometric area. This is why we must look at the sign of the function before deciding whether a geometric area interpretation is appropriate.

exercises.question: Evaluate $\int_{-2}^{2} x^3\,dx$ using symmetry or another efficient method. Explain why your method is appropriate.
exercises.solution:
The function $f(x)=x^3$ is odd, meaning $f(-x)=-f(x)$. Over a symmetric interval $[-a,a]$, the integral of an odd function is $0$ because the contributions on the left and right cancel.

So,
$$\int_{-2}^{2} x^3\,dx=0.$$ 

This is a geometric/symmetry-based approach, which is efficient here because the problem has a symmetric interval and an odd integrand.

content.summary: The definite integral begins as a limit of Riemann sums, so its meaning is accumulated signed area rather than an abstract symbol. Antiderivatives provide the reverse operation to differentiation, and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus connects the two halves of the subject: Part 1 says an accumulation function differentiates back to its integrand, while Part 2 says a definite integral equals an antiderivative evaluated at the endpoints. With these tools, basic definite integrals become endpoint computations, and variable-limit accumulation functions become differentiable objects whose derivatives reveal the original rate.
content.nextSteps: Next, practice translating words into integrals and deciding whether a problem is best handled by geometry, Riemann-sum reasoning, or the Fundamental Theorem. Then move on to techniques of integration, where the main challenge becomes finding antiderivatives for more complicated functions.

flashcards.front: What is the definite integral defined as?
flashcards.back: The limit of Riemann sums: $\displaystyle\int_a^b f(x)\,dx=\lim_{\|P\|\to0}\sum_{i} f(x_i^*)\Delta x_i$, when the partition mesh $\|P\|$ goes to zero.

flashcards.front: Does the definite integral always equal geometric area?
flashcards.back: No — the integral measures net accumulation: regions where $f(x)>0$ add positive contribution and regions where $f(x)<0$ add negative contribution; geometric area ignores sign.

flashcards.front: How do values of $f(x)$ below the $x$-axis affect the integral?
flashcards.back: They contribute negative signed area, reducing the value of the definite integral (they subtract from net accumulation).

flashcards.front: What regularity condition is needed for the standard Fundamental Theorem statements?
flashcards.back: Continuity of $f$ on the interval (or at least integrability plus local conditions) is the usual hypothesis ensuring $A(x)=\int_a^x f(t)\,dt$ is differentiable and the evaluation theorem applies.

flashcards.front: What is an antiderivative of $f$?
flashcards.back: A function $F$ such that $F'(x)=f(x)$ on the interval.

flashcards.front: What does the indefinite integral $\int f(x)\,dx$ represent?
flashcards.back: The family of all antiderivatives of $f$: $\int f(x)\,dx=F(x)+C$, where $F'(x)=f(x)$ and $C$ is an arbitrary constant.

flashcards.front: Why do we include a constant $+C$ with indefinite integrals?
flashcards.back: Because any two antiderivatives differ by a constant, so $+C$ accounts for all vertical shifts that still differentiate to $f$.

flashcards.front: How do you check that a proposed antiderivative is correct?
flashcards.back: Differentiate it: if $\frac{d}{dx}F(x)=f(x)$ on the interval, then $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$.

flashcards.front: State Part 1 of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
flashcards.back: If $A(x)=\int_a^x f(t)\,dt$ with $f$ continuous, then $A'(x)=f(x)$: the derivative of an accumulation function recovers the integrand.

flashcards.front: How does a small increase in the upper limit change an accumulation function?
flashcards.back: A small increase $dx$ adds approximately $f(x)\,dx$ to the integral — the area of a thin vertical slice — so $dA\approx f(x)\,dx$.

flashcards.front: State Part 2 (the evaluation theorem) of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
flashcards.back: If $F$ is any antiderivative of continuous $f$ on $[a,b]$, then $\displaystyle\int_a^b f(x)\,dx=F(b)-F(a)$. Any constant of integration cancels when evaluating the bounds.

quiz.question: Suppose a continuous function f satisfies f(x)<0 for -2<x<0 and f(x)>0 for 0<x<3. Which best describes the value of $\int_{-2}^{3} f(x)\,dx$?
options: The total area under the curve between -2 and 3, ignoring sign.
The net signed area: positive area on (0,3) minus absolute area on (-2,0).
Only the area where f(x)>0 (the negative part is discarded).
The average value of f on [-2,3].
quiz.explanation: A definite integral sums signed contributions, so it gives the net signed area (positive parts add, negative parts subtract). The first and third choices are wrong because they ignore sign; the fourth is a different quantity (integral divided by interval length).

quiz.question: Let $F(x)=\int_{0}^{x} f(t)\,dt$ where $f$ is continuous. According to the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Part 1), what is $F'(x)$?
quiz.explanation: FTC Part 1 states that the derivative of the accumulation function $F(x)=\int_{0}^{x} f(t)\,dt$ is $f(x)$ when $f$ is continuous. The other options confuse differentiation of $f$ or reuse $F$ itself and are not what the theorem gives.

quiz.question: Compute $\int_{0}^{\pi} \sin x\,dx$ using an antiderivative and the Fundamental Theorem (Part 2).
quiz.explanation: An antiderivative of $\sin x$ is $-\cos x$, so evaluate $[-\cos x]_0^{\pi}=(-\cos\pi)-(-\cos0)= -(-1)-(-1)=2$. The integral is not 0 or negative because $\sin x$ is positive on $(0,\pi)$, and 1 is simply incorrect arithmetic.

quiz.question: Let $G(x)=\int_{1}^{x^{2}} \ln t\,dt$. What is $G'(x)$ for $x\neq 0$?
quiz.explanation: By the chain rule with FTC, $G'(x)=\ln(x^{2})\cdot (x^{2})' = \ln(x^{2})\cdot 2x$. Option 1 is correct; option 2 drops the factor $x$, option 3 omits the derivative of the upper limit, and option 4 incorrectly adds a constant (derivatives don't produce arbitrary constants).

quiz.question: Which approach is the quickest and most natural to evaluate $\int_{-1}^{1} \sqrt{1-x^{2}}\,dx$?
options: Recognize the integrand as the upper half of a unit circle and use geometry (area of a semicircle).
Directly find an elementary antiderivative of $\sqrt{1-x^{2}}$ without substitution.
Estimate by a Riemann sum numerically.
Apply the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus using a simple closed-form antiderivative of $\sqrt{1-x^{2}}$.
quiz.explanation: The graph of $y=\sqrt{1-x^{2}}$ is the top half of the unit circle, so the integral equals the semicircle area $\tfrac{1}{2}\pi(1)^{2}=\tfrac{\pi}{2}$. Finding a direct elementary antiderivative requires trig substitution (so option 1 is simpler), numerical Riemann sums are unnecessary, and there is no simple algebraic antiderivative without substitution.

quiz.question: If $f$ is an odd integrable function on $[-a,a]$, what is $\int_{-a}^{a} f(x)\,dx$?
options: It may be undefined unless f is continuous.
quiz.explanation: Odd functions satisfy $f(-x)=-f(x)$, so areas on symmetric intervals cancel and the integral is 0. Option 1 is correct; option 2 would be true for even functions, option 3 is generally false, and option 4 is wrong because integrability (not full continuity) suffices for the integral to exist.

quiz.question: Let $H(x)=\int_{x}^{x^{2}} e^{t^{2}}\,dt$. What is $H'(x)$?
quiz.explanation: Differentiate using the Leibniz rule: derivative equals $e^{(x^{2})^{2}}\cdot (x^{2})' - e^{x^{2}}\cdot (x)' = 2x e^{x^{4}} - e^{x^{2}}$. The other choices have the terms swapped or negated incorrectly, and the derivative is not zero because the limits depend on $x$.

chapterData.title: Techniques of Integration I: Substitution and Algebraic Forms
chapterData.description: Develop substitution as the inverse of the chain rule, then use it to simplify integrals involving composite functions and rational expressions.
learningGoals: Recognize when a substitution matches an inner derivative.
Choose substitutions that reduce an integral to a standard form.
Integrate algebraic and exponential expressions after substitution.
Check an antiderivative by differentiating it back to the integrand.
Set up and evaluate substitution-based definite integrals carefully under change of variables.
topics: Change of variables in definite integrals
Integrals involving powers of linear functions
Worked examples and verification by differentiation

content.introduction:
Integration becomes much more manageable once you learn to recognize when an integrand is really a composite function in disguise. In differentiation, the chain rule tells us how to differentiate something like $f(g(x))$; in integration, $u$-substitution works in the opposite direction. The central skill in this chapter is pattern recognition: spotting an inner expression and its derivative, then rewriting the integral in a simpler variable.

This chapter focuses on the algebraic mechanics of substitution and the reasoning behind it. You will learn how to choose a substitution, how to interpret the differential $du$ correctly, and how to carry the method through both indefinite and definite integrals. We will also look at common patterns involving powers of linear functions, exponentials, and logarithmic forms, along with the most frequent mistakes students make when the substitution is chosen well but executed carelessly.

sections.title: 1. $u$-Substitution as the Reverse of the Chain Rule
sections.content:
The basic idea of substitution is simple: if an integrand contains a function and its derivative, we can rename the inner function by a new variable $u$ and reduce the integral to a standard form. This is not a trick; it is the reverse of the chain rule. If the chain rule says
$$\frac{d}{dx}F(g(x))=F'(g(x))g'(x),$$
then substitution says that an integral of the form $\int F'(g(x))g'(x)\,dx$ can be rewritten as $\int F'(u)\,du$.

The most important judgment is deciding what should count as the inner expression. Usually, we look for a composite function $g(x)$ whose derivative $g'(x)$ appears elsewhere in the integrand, perhaps up to a constant multiple. When this happens, the differential changes cleanly: if $u=g(x)$, then $du=g'(x)\,dx$. That relation is the engine of the method.

Worked example: evaluate $\int 2x\cos(x^2)\,dx$. We look for an inner function whose derivative appears. Since the cosine contains $x^2$, let $u=x^2$.
$$u=x^2 \quad \text{(choose the inner expression)}$$
$$du=2x\,dx \quad \text{(differentiate both sides)}$$
Now rewrite the integral in $u$:
$$\int 2x\cos(x^2)\,dx=\int \cos(u)\,du \quad \text{(replace $x^2$ by $u$ and $2x\,dx$ by $du$)}$$
Integrate:
$$\int \cos(u)\,du=\sin(u)+C \quad \text{(standard antiderivative)}$$
Substitute back:
$$\sin(u)+C=\sin(x^2)+C \quad \text{(return to $x$)}$$
So the result is $\sin(x^2)+C$.

A useful habit is to verify your answer by differentiating it. If we differentiate $\sin(x^2)+C$, the chain rule gives $\cos(x^2)\cdot 2x$, which matches the original integrand. This check is especially valuable early on, because it confirms both the substitution and the final antiderivative.
keyPoints: Substitution reverses the chain rule: $\int F'(g(x))g'(x)\,dx=\int F'(u)\,du$.
Choose $u$ to be the inner expression whose derivative appears in the integrand.
The differential $du$ stands for the derivative times $dx$, for example $du=2x\,dx$ when $u=x^2$.
Always substitute back to $x$ unless the problem asks for the answer in terms of $u$.
Differentiate your antiderivative to confirm that it reproduces the integrand.

sections.title: 2. Interpreting $du$ and Choosing Substitutions Carefully
sections.content:
Students often think of $du$ as a symbol they can move around freely. That intuition is useful, but it must be handled carefully: $du$ is not just a decorative letter. It represents the change in the new variable, and in substitution it replaces the differential part of the original integral after the derivative relation has been established.

The disciplined way to use substitution is this: first define $u=g(x)$, then compute $du=g'(x)\,dx$, then solve that relation for the exact expression appearing in the integrand. If a constant multiple is present, it can be absorbed algebraically. If the derivative is only partially present, you may need to factor a constant out or rewrite the integrand before substituting.

Worked example: evaluate $\int (4x+1)^7\,dx$. The integrand is a power of a linear function. Let
$$u=4x+1 \quad \text{(inner linear expression)}$$
$$du=4\,dx \quad \text{(differentiate)}$$
Solve for $dx$:
$$dx=\frac{1}{4}\,du \quad \text{(rewrite the differential)}$$
Now substitute:
$$\int (4x+1)^7\,dx=\int u^7\left(\frac{1}{4}\,du\right) \quad \text{(replace the inner expression)}$$
$$=\frac14\int u^7\,du \quad \text{(factor out the constant)}$$
$$=\frac14\cdot \frac{u^8}{8}+C \quad \text{(power rule)}$$
$$=\frac{u^8}{32}+C \quad \text{(simplify)}$$
Return to $x$:
$$\frac{(4x+1)^8}{32}+C$$
To verify, differentiate:
$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{(4x+1)^8}{32}\right)=\frac{8(4x+1)^7\cdot 4}{32}=(4x+1)^7,$$
which matches the integrand.

The key lesson is that substitution is not guessing at random; it is a structured rewrite. If the derivative of your proposed $u$ does not appear in the integrand, the substitution is probably not the right one, or the integrand may need algebraic manipulation first.
keyPoints: Define $u=g(x)$ first, then compute $du=g'(x)\,dx$ before rewriting the integral.
If $du$ differs by a constant factor, isolate that factor algebraically.
Powers of linear expressions are often ideal candidates for substitution.
$du$ is a relation, not a freely chosen new symbol; it must come from differentiating $u$.
Check the final answer by differentiating it back to the original integrand.

sections.title: 3. Powers of Linear Functions and Algebraic Forms
sections.content:
Many integrals in calculus II become straightforward once a linear expression is isolated. A power of a linear function, such as $(ax+b)^n$, is a standard pattern because its derivative is just the constant $a$. The same idea works for many algebraic forms: roots, reciprocals, and rational powers often simplify after a linear substitution.

The reason this works is that the derivative of a linear function does not introduce any new variable dependence. That means after setting $u=ax+b$, the differential $du=a\,dx$ contributes only a constant factor, and the remaining integral becomes a simple power of $u$. Once the integrand is in the form $u^n$, the power rule applies directly unless $n=-1$.

Worked example: evaluate $\int \frac{x}{(x^2+1)^3}\,dx$. The denominator suggests the inner function $x^2+1$, and the numerator $x\,dx$ is close to its derivative. Let
$$u=x^2+1 \quad \text{(choose the denominator’s inner expression)}$$
$$du=2x\,dx \quad \text{(differentiate)}$$
Solve for the piece in the integral:
$$x\,dx=\frac12\,du \quad \text{(match the differential)}$$
Substitute:
$$\int \frac{x}{(x^2+1)^3}\,dx=\int \frac{1}{u^3}\left(\frac12\,du\right) \quad \text{(rewrite everything in $u$)}$$
$$=\frac12\int u^{-3}\,du \quad \text{(use exponent notation)}$$
$$=\frac12\cdot \frac{u^{-2}}{-2}+C \quad \text{(power rule)}$$
$$=-\frac14u^{-2}+C \quad \text{(simplify)}$$
Return to $x$:
$$-\frac{1}{4(x^2+1)^2}+C$$
Verification by differentiation gives
$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(-\frac{1}{4(x^2+1)^2}\right)= -\frac14\cdot (-2)(x^2+1)^{-3}\cdot 2x=\frac{x}{(x^2+1)^3},$$
so the answer is correct.

A second useful algebraic form is the power of a binomial with a reciprocal factor, such as $\frac{1}{(ax+b)^n}$. These often become especially easy after substitution because the reciprocal turns into a negative exponent. Treating all algebraic forms uniformly through exponent notation helps keep the method consistent and reduces special-case thinking.
keyPoints: Linear expressions $ax+b$ are especially friendly because $du=a\,dx$ is constant.
Rewrite roots and reciprocals using exponents, such as $(x^2+1)^{-3}$, to apply the power rule cleanly.
Look for a factor in the integrand that matches the derivative of the inner expression.
A successful substitution often turns a complicated algebraic integrand into $u^n$.
Verify algebraic-substitution answers by differentiating the result.

sections.title: 4. Exponential and Logarithmic Substitutions
sections.content:
Substitution is especially powerful when the integrand contains exponential growth or logarithmic structure. Exponentials often come with their own derivative, which makes them natural candidates for $u$. Logarithms frequently appear in denominators or inside other functions, and their derivatives can provide the exact $1/x$ factor needed for substitution.

A helpful strategy is to recognize the derivative pattern before trying to integrate directly. For example, the derivative of $e^{g(x)}$ is $e^{g(x)}g'(x)$, so if the integrand contains $e^{g(x)}$ times something like $g'(x)$, substitution is immediate. Likewise, the derivative of $\ln|g(x)|$ is $g'(x)/g(x)$, so expressions with a numerator matching the derivative of the denominator are often designed for logarithmic antiderivatives.

Worked example: evaluate $\int \frac{e^{3x}}{1+e^{3x}}\,dx$. Let the exponential expression be the new variable:
$$u=1+e^{3x} \quad \text{(choose the denominator)}$$
Differentiate:
$$du=3e^{3x}\,dx \quad \text{(compute the derivative)}$$
Solve for the factor in the integrand:
$$e^{3x}\,dx=\frac13\,du \quad \text{(isolate the matching piece)}$$
Now substitute:
$$\int \frac{e^{3x}}{1+e^{3x}}\,dx=\int \frac{1}{u}\left(\frac13\,du\right) \quad \text{(rewrite in $u$)}$$
$$=\frac13\int \frac{1}{u}\,du \quad \text{(simplify)}$$
$$=\frac13\ln|u|+C \quad \text{(logarithmic antiderivative)}$$
Return to $x$:
$$\frac13\ln|1+e^{3x}|+C$$
Differentiate to verify:
$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac13\ln|1+e^{3x}|\right)=\frac13\cdot \frac{3e^{3x}}{1+e^{3x}}=\frac{e^{3x}}{1+e^{3x}},$$
so the result is correct.

Logarithmic substitution also appears in integrals like $\int \frac{g'(x)}{g(x)}\,dx$. When you see a numerator that is the derivative of the denominator, the antiderivative is often a logarithm. This is one of the most important patterns in integration because it turns a complicated rational-looking expression into a standard formula.
keyPoints: For exponentials, look for $e^{g(x)}g'(x)$ before attempting anything more complicated.
For logarithmic forms, look for $g'(x)/g(x)$, which integrates to $\ln|g(x)|+C$.
Substitution often turns an exponential or logarithmic integral into $\int \frac{1}{u}\,du$ or $\int e^u\,du$.
Absolute values matter in logarithmic antiderivatives: $\ln|u|+C$.
Always check by differentiating, especially when logs are involved.

sections.title: 5. Definite Integrals Under Change of Variables and Common Pitfalls
sections.content:
For definite integrals, substitution changes not only the integrand but also the bounds. This is one of the clearest places to see the meaning of change of variables: if $u=g(x)$, then every $x$-value at the endpoints corresponds to a $u$-value. You can either transform the bounds and integrate entirely in $u$, or integrate in $u$ and then substitute back to $x$ before evaluating. The first method is usually cleaner.

Worked example: evaluate $\int_0^1 2x\,\sqrt{1+x^2}\,dx$. Choose
$$u=1+x^2 \quad \text{(inner expression under the root)}$$
$$du=2x\,dx \quad \text{(match the outside factor)}$$
Change the bounds:
- when $x=0$, $u=1+0^2=1$
- when $x=1$, $u=1+1^2=2$
Now rewrite the integral:
$$\int_0^1 2x\sqrt{1+x^2}\,dx=\int_1^2 \sqrt{u}\,du \quad \text{(substitute and change bounds)}$$
$$=\int_1^2 u^{1/2}\,du \quad \text{(write as a power)}$$
$$=\left[\frac{2}{3}u^{3/2}\right]_1^2 \quad \text{(antiderivative)}$$
$$=\frac{2}{3}(2^{3/2}-1^{3/2}) \quad \text{(evaluate at the bounds)}$$
So the value is $\frac{2}{3}(2\sqrt2-1)$.

The main pitfalls are conceptual rather than computational. First, do not change $dx$ to $du$ without establishing the derivative relation. Second, remember to transform the limits if you choose to stay in $u$; otherwise, you must change back to $x$ before using the original limits. Third, if the integrand does not contain the derivative of your chosen inner function, do not force the substitution. It may require algebraic rewriting, or a different method entirely.

Another common mistake is forgetting the constant factor created by $du$. For example, if $u=5x-1$, then $du=5\,dx$ and $dx=\frac15du$. Dropping that factor changes the answer. A final good practice is to differentiate your result when the integral is indefinite, or to estimate the definite integral’s sign and size to see whether your final numerical answer is plausible.
keyPoints: For definite integrals, either change the bounds or substitute back before evaluating; do not mix the two approaches.
The bounds must be converted using the same substitution $u=g(x)$.
Never replace $dx$ with $du$ unless you have derived the relation $du=g'(x)\,dx$.
Constant factors from $du$ must be preserved; missing them gives the wrong answer.
If substitution feels forced, pause and look for algebraic rewriting or a different method.

exercises.question: Use substitution to evaluate the integral $\int 2x\cos(x^2)\,dx$. Identify the substitution, rewrite the integral, and then convert back to $x$.
exercises.solution: Let $u=x^2$. Then $du=2x\,dx$, which matches the factor already in the integral. So the integral becomes $$\int 2x\cos(x^2)\,dx=\int \cos(u)\,du.$$ The antiderivative of $\cos(u)$ is $\sin(u)+C$, so $$\int 2x\cos(x^2)\,dx=\sin(u)+C=\sin(x^2)+C.$$ Check by differentiating: $$\frac{d}{dx}[\sin(x^2)] = \cos(x^2)\cdot 2x,$$ which matches the original integrand.

exercises.question: Which substitution is most natural for the integral $\int (3x-1)^7\,dx$? After choosing it, evaluate the integral completely.
exercises.solution: A natural choice is $u=3x-1$ because its derivative is a constant multiple of the factor $dx$. Then $du=3\,dx$, so $dx=\frac{1}{3}du$. Substitute: $$\int (3x-1)^7\,dx = \int u^7\cdot \frac{1}{3}\,du = \frac{1}{3}\int u^7\,du.$$ Now integrate: $$\frac{1}{3}\cdot \frac{u^8}{8}+C = \frac{u^8}{24}+C.$$ Substitute back: $$\int (3x-1)^7\,dx = \frac{(3x-1)^8}{24}+C.$$ Check: differentiating gives $$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{(3x-1)^8}{24}\right)=\frac{8(3x-1)^7\cdot 3}{24}=(3x-1)^7,$$ as required.

exercises.question: Evaluate the integral $\int \frac{5x^4}{x^5+2}\,dx$ using substitution. Explain why the substitution works and verify your answer by differentiation.
exercises.solution: Let $u=x^5+2$. Then $du=5x^4\,dx$, which appears exactly in the integrand. The integral becomes $$\int \frac{5x^4}{x^5+2}\,dx = \int \frac{1}{u}\,du.$$ Since $\int \frac{1}{u}\,du=\ln|u|+C$, we get $$\int \frac{5x^4}{x^5+2}\,dx=\ln|x^5+2|+C.$$ Check by differentiating: $$\frac{d}{dx}[\ln|x^5+2|]=\frac{1}{x^5+2}\cdot 5x^4=\frac{5x^4}{x^5+2},$$ which matches the integrand.

exercises.question: A classmate says: "If I want to use substitution, I should always choose the most complicated-looking part of the integrand." Reflect on why this is not a reliable rule. Give one example where a simpler choice of $u$ is better, and explain what you would look for instead.
exercises.solution:
That rule is not reliable because the best substitution is not the most complicated expression; it is the one whose derivative is already present, up to a constant factor. The goal is to transform the integral into a standard form like $\int u^n\,du$, $\int e^u\,du$, or $\int \frac{1}{u}\,du$.

For example, in $$\int x(1+x^2)^4\,dx,$$ the most complicated-looking part is $(1+x^2)^4$, but the better choice is $u=1+x^2$, because then $du=2x\,dx$, and the integrand contains $x\,dx$. That gives $$\int x(1+x^2)^4\,dx = \frac{1}{2}\int u^4\,du,$$ which is easy to integrate.

What I would look for instead is an inner expression whose derivative appears nearby in the integrand. I would also check whether the substitution turns the integral into a familiar algebraic, exponential, or logarithmic form. In short, choose the substitution that simplifies the structure, not the one that merely looks most complex.

exercises.question: Evaluate the definite integral $\int_{0}^{1} 2x e^{x^2}\,dx$ using substitution. Be careful to transform the limits correctly and state the final numerical value.
exercises.solution:
Let $u=x^2$. Then $du=2x\,dx$. Since this is a definite integral, change the limits too:
- when $x=0$, $u=0^2=0$;
- when $x=1$, $u=1^2=1$.

So the integral becomes $$\int_{0}^{1} 2x e^{x^2}\,dx = \int_{0}^{1} e^u\,du.$$ Integrate: $$\int_{0}^{1} e^u\,du = \left.e^u\right|_0^1 = e-1.$$ Therefore, $$\int_{0}^{1} 2x e^{x^2}\,dx = e-1.$$ If we instead converted back to $x$, we would get $e^{x^2}$ as an antiderivative, and evaluating from $0$ to $1$ also gives $e-1$.

exercises.question: Find an antiderivative of $\int \frac{4x}{(x^2+1)^3}\,dx$ and then verify your result by differentiating it back to the integrand.
exercises.solution: Choose $u=x^2+1$. Then $du=2x\,dx$, so $4x\,dx=2\,du$. The integral becomes $$\int \frac{4x}{(x^2+1)^3}\,dx = \int 2u^{-3}\,du.$$ Apply the power rule: $$\int 2u^{-3}\,du = 2\cdot \frac{u^{-2}}{-2}+C = -u^{-2}+C.$$ Substitute back: $$\int \frac{4x}{(x^2+1)^3}\,dx = -\frac{1}{(x^2+1)^2}+C.$$ Check by differentiating: $$\frac{d}{dx}\left[-(x^2+1)^{-2}\right] = -(-2)(x^2+1)^{-3}\cdot 2x = \frac{4x}{(x^2+1)^3},$$ which matches the original integrand.

content.summary: Substitution is the integration counterpart to the chain rule: once you identify an inner function and its derivative, many integrals collapse to familiar forms in a new variable. In this chapter you learned how to select $u$, interpret $du$ correctly, handle powers of linear expressions, and integrate exponential and logarithmic forms. You also saw how definite integrals transform under change of variables and why verifying answers by differentiation is such a powerful safeguard.
content.nextSteps: Next, use substitution repeatedly on mixed practice sets until choosing $u$ becomes automatic. Then move on to integration by parts, where the goal shifts from reversing the chain rule to reversing the product rule.

flashcards.front: What identity expresses substitution as the inverse of the chain rule?
flashcards.back: If $u=g(x)$ then $\int F'(g(x))\,g'(x)\,dx=\int F'(u)\,du=F(u)+C$, with $u=g(x)$.

flashcards.front: What are the standard steps for performing a $u$-substitution?
flashcards.back: 1) Let $u=g(x)$. 2) Compute $du=g'(x)\,dx$. 3) Rewrite the integral in $u$ and $du$. 4) Integrate with respect to $u$. 5) Substitute back to $x$ (or convert bounds if definite).

flashcards.front: How do you handle $du$ when it differs by a constant factor from $dx$ in the integrand?
flashcards.back: If $du=a\,dx$ then $dx=du/a$, so factor the constant: $\int f(x)\,dx=\int f(u)\frac{du}{a}=(1/a)\int f(u)\,du$; never drop the constant.

flashcards.front: Why must $du$ come from differentiating $u=g(x)$ rather than be chosen arbitrarily?
flashcards.back: $du$ is the differential relation $du=g'(x)\,dx$ that follows from $u=g(x)$; replacing $dx$ by $du$ is valid only after deriving this relation.

flashcards.front: When is $u$ best chosen as the inner expression of a composite function?
flashcards.back: Choose $u$ equal to the inner expression when its derivative (or a constant multiple) appears in the integrand; this turns a composite integrand into a simpler integral in $u$.

flashcards.front: What substitution handles integrals of the form $\int \frac{g'(x)}{g(x)}\,dx$?
flashcards.back: Let $u=g(x)$. Then $\int \frac{g'(x)}{g(x)}\,dx=\int \frac{1}{u}\,du=\ln|u|+C=\ln|g(x)|+C$.

flashcards.front: What substitution handles integrals of the form $\int e^{g(x)}g'(x)\,dx$?
flashcards.back: Let $u=g(x)$. Then $\int e^{g(x)}g'(x)\,dx=\int e^u\,du=e^u+C=e^{g(x)}+C$.

flashcards.front: What must you do when using substitution in a definite integral?
flashcards.back: Either change the bounds using $u=g(a)$ and $u=g(b)$ and integrate in $u$, or perform the antiderivative in $u$ then substitute back to $x$ before evaluating; do not mix the two approaches.

flashcards.front: Why are linear expressions $ax+b$ particularly convenient for substitution?
flashcards.back: Because $du=a\,dx$ is constant, so substitution only introduces a constant factor that can be factored out, simplifying powers of $ax+b$ to $u^n$.

flashcards.front: Why rewrite roots and reciprocals as exponents before integrating after substitution?
flashcards.back: Rewrite expressions like $\sqrt{\,\cdot\,}$ or $1/(\cdot)$ as $(\cdot)^{1/2}$ or $(\cdot)^{-1}$ so you can apply the power rule $\int u^n\,du= u^{n+1}/(n+1)+C$ (for $n\neq-1$).

flashcards.front: How should you check an antiderivative obtained by substitution?
flashcards.back: Differentiate the antiderivative and verify it equals the original integrand; check absolute values in logarithmic results and constant factors from $du$.

quiz.question: Which substitution most directly simplifies the integral ∫ x cos(x^2) dx?
quiz.explanation: Let u = x^2 so du = 2x dx and the integrand becomes (1/2) cos u du, which is a standard integral. The other choices do not produce du proportional to x dx (so they don't remove the x factor): u = sin x or u = cos x have du = cos x dx or du = -sin x dx, and u = x changes nothing useful.

quiz.question: Evaluate ∫ (3x+1)^5 dx using a substitution.
quiz.explanation: Take u = 3x+1 so du = 3 dx, hence dx = du/3 and ∫(3x+1)^5 dx = (1/3)∫u^5 du = (1/3)(1/6)u^6 = (1/18)u^6 + C. The other answers result from forgetting or misapplying the factor from du = 3 dx.

quiz.question: Find an antiderivative of e^{2x}.
quiz.explanation: With u = 2x, du = 2 dx so ∫e^{2x} dx = (1/2)∫e^{u} du = (1/2)e^{u} + C = (1/2)e^{2x} + C. The other choices either ignore the 2 from the chain rule or apply it incorrectly to the exponent.

quiz.question: Compute ∫ (2x)/(x^2+1) dx.
quiz.explanation: With u = x^2+1 we have du = 2x dx, so the integral is ∫ du/u = ln|u| + C = ln(x^2+1) + C. The (1/2) factor is wrong because we don't need it here; ln|x| and arctan(x) are antiderivatives of different rational forms.

quiz.question: Evaluate the definite integral ∫_0^1 2x e^{x^2} dx by substitution.
quiz.explanation: Let u = x^2 so du = 2x dx and when x runs 0→1, u runs 0→1. The integral becomes ∫_0^1 e^{u} du = e^1 - e^0 = e - 1. The other options reflect incorrect factors or sign errors in the change of variables.

quiz.question: Which substitution simplifies ∫ (x+1)/(x^2+2x+2) dx most directly?
quiz.explanation: The derivative of u = x^2+2x+2 is du = (2x+2)dx = 2(x+1)dx, so (x+1)dx = du/2 and the integral becomes (1/2)∫ du/u. Taking u = x+1 does not produce the denominator's derivative, and u = (x+1)^2+1 or u = x^2 similarly fail to match du to the numerator without extra manipulation.

quiz.question: Differentiate F(x) = ln(x^2+1). Which of the following is F'(x)?
quiz.explanation: By the chain rule, d/dx ln(x^2+1) = (1/(x^2+1))·(2x) = 2x/(x^2+1). The other choices omit or misplace the derivative of the inner function $x^2+1$, which is 2x.

chapterData.title: Techniques of Integration II: Integration by Parts and Trigonometric Integrals
chapterData.description: Use the product rule in reverse to handle products of functions, then manage trigonometric powers and products with identities and systematic reduction strategies.
learningGoals: Derive the integration by parts formula from the product rule.
Select effective choices for $u$ and $dv$ in common problems.
Evaluate integrals involving products of polynomials, exponentials, logarithms, and trig functions.
Reduce trigonometric integrals using identities and parity arguments.
Handle repeated integration by parts and recognize cyclic integrals.
topics: Integrals of $x^n e^x$, $x^n\sin x$, and $x^n\ln x$
Products of sine and cosine with different frequencies

content.introduction:
Integration by parts is the first major place in Calculus II where an idea from differentiation is turned around and used as a tool for integration. If the product rule tells us how derivatives of products behave, then integration by parts tells us how to strategically move a derivative from one factor to another so that the integral becomes easier. This is not a memorized trick; it is a direct consequence of the product rule, and understanding that origin makes the method much easier to use correctly.

The second major theme of this chapter is that trigonometric integrals become manageable once we learn to look for structure: parity, identities, and patterns in powers. Some integrals are simplified by a careful choice of $u$ and $dv$; others yield to identities such as $\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1$ or power-reduction formulas. The goal of this chapter is not merely to compute a collection of integrals, but to develop a reliable strategy for deciding which transformation will reduce a hard integral to a simpler one.

sections.title: 1. Deriving Integration by Parts from the Product Rule
sections.content:
The product rule says that if $u=u(x)$ and $v=v(x)$ are differentiable, then $\frac{d}{dx}(uv)=u\,v'+v\,u'$. The key idea behind integration by parts is to integrate both sides of this identity. Since integration is the inverse of differentiation, we expect that an antiderivative of $u\,v'+v\,u'$ should be $uv$; rearranging the terms gives a formula for one product integral in terms of another.

To derive the formula cleanly, begin with
$$\frac{d}{dx}(uv)=u\,\frac{dv}{dx}+v\,\frac{du}{dx}.$$ 
Integrate both sides with respect to $x$:
$$\int \frac{d}{dx}(uv)\,dx=\int u\,\frac{dv}{dx}\,dx+\int v\,\frac{du}{dx}\,dx.$$ 
The left side simplifies to $uv$, so
$$uv=\int u\,dv+\int v\,du.$$ 
Solving for the first integral gives the standard formula
$$\int u\,dv=uv-\int v\,du.$$ 
This is the core rule of integration by parts.

Now we work a complete example. Evaluate $\int x e^x\,dx$.

Set
$$u=x, \qquad dv=e^x\,dx.$$ 
Then
$$du=dx, \qquad v=\int e^x\,dx=e^x.$$ 
Apply the formula:
$$\int x e^x\,dx=uv-\int v\,du$$
$$=x e^x-\int e^x\,dx \quad \text{(substitute $u,v,du$)}$$
$$=x e^x-e^x+C \quad \text{(integrate the remaining exponential)}$$
$$=e^x(x-1)+C.$$ 
The result is $\int x e^x\,dx=e^x(x-1)+C$.

A useful intuition is that integration by parts trades one derivative for one integral. If one factor becomes simpler when differentiated, and the other factor remains easy to integrate, then the method is likely to help. This is why polynomials often pair well with exponentials or trigonometric functions: differentiating the polynomial reduces its degree, while integrating the other factor is straightforward.
keyPoints: Integration by parts comes directly from the product rule.
The formula is $\int u\,dv=uv-\int v\,du$.
Choose $u$ to simplify when differentiated, and choose $dv$ to be easy to integrate.
The method is especially effective for products involving polynomials, exponentials, logarithms, and trigonometric functions.

sections.title: 2. Choosing $u$ and $dv$ Strategically; Tabular Integration
sections.content:
The formula alone does not solve a problem until we choose $u$ and $dv$ wisely. A good choice of $u$ is one that becomes simpler after differentiation. A good choice of $dv$ is one that can be integrated without difficulty. In practice, this often means taking $u$ to be a logarithm or polynomial and $dv$ to be an exponential, sine, cosine, or simple power of $x$.

A helpful rule of thumb is often summarized by the preference list LIATE: logarithmic, inverse trigonometric, algebraic, trigonometric, exponential. It is not a theorem, but it often guides a good choice. For example, in $\int x\sin x\,dx$, choosing $u=x$ and $dv=\sin x\,dx$ is natural because $x$ simplifies when differentiated and $\sin x$ integrates to $-\cos x$. If we chose the opposite, the integral would not become simpler.

Let us work a complete example: evaluate $\int x\sin x\,dx$.

Choose
$$u=x, \qquad dv=\sin x\,dx.$$ 
Then
$$du=dx, \qquad v=\int \sin x\,dx=-\cos x.$$ 
Apply the formula:
$$\int x\sin x\,dx=uv-\int v\,du$$
$$=x(-\cos x)-\int (-\cos x)\,dx \quad \text{(substitute $u,v,du$)}$$
$$=-x\cos x+\int \cos x\,dx \quad \text{(simplify signs)}$$
$$=-x\cos x+\sin x+C.$$ 
So $\int x\sin x\,dx=-x\cos x+\sin x+C$.

When repeated integration by parts is needed, a tabular method can save time. This method is especially effective for integrals like $\int x^3 e^x\,dx$ or $\int x^2\sin x\,dx$, where repeated differentiation of the polynomial eventually reaches zero. The table records repeated derivatives of the algebraic factor and repeated antiderivatives of the other factor; then one combines entries along diagonals with alternating signs.

Here is a complete tabular example for $\int x^2 e^x\,dx$.

Differentiate $x^2$ until zero and integrate $e^x$ repeatedly:

$$\begin{array}{c|c}
\text{Derivatives of }x^2 & \text{Antiderivatives of }e^x \\\hline
x^2 & e^x \\
2x & e^x \\
2 & e^x \\
0 & e^x
\end{array}$$

Now pair diagonally with alternating signs:
$$\int x^2 e^x\,dx = x^2 e^x - (2x)e^x + 2e^x + C.$$ 
Factor if desired:
$$\int x^2 e^x\,dx = e^x(x^2-2x+2)+C.$$ 
The table is simply a compact way to perform integration by parts repeatedly until the polynomial vanishes.

The same idea works for $x^n\sin x$ and $x^n\cos x$. The essential pattern is that each application lowers the power of $x$ by one, so after finitely many steps the process ends. That is why these integrals are systematic rather than ad hoc.
examples: Compute $\int x^2 e^x\,dx$ by tabular integration.
keyPoints: Choose $u$ so that differentiation simplifies it.
Choose $dv$ so that it is easy to integrate.
Tabular integration is efficient for polynomials times $e^x$, $\sin x$, or $\cos x$.
Repeated integration by parts terminates when the polynomial degree reaches zero.

sections.title: 3. Integrals Involving $x^n e^x$, $x^n\sin x$, and $x^n\ln x$
sections.content:
Integrals involving a polynomial times $e^x$ or a trigonometric function are classical candidates for repeated integration by parts. Each differentiation lowers the polynomial degree, so after finitely many steps the remaining integral is elementary. This is why $\int x^n e^x\,dx$ and $\int x^n\sin x\,dx$ can always be reduced to combinations of lower-degree integrals.

Let us derive a useful recurrence for $\int x^n e^x\,dx$. Set
$$I_n=\int x^n e^x\,dx.$$ 
Use integration by parts with $u=x^n$ and $dv=e^x\,dx$:
$$u=x^n, \qquad du=n x^{n-1}\,dx, \qquad v=e^x.$$ 
Then
$$I_n=x^n e^x-\int n x^{n-1}e^x\,dx$$
$$=x^n e^x-nI_{n-1}.$$ 
So the recurrence is
$$I_n=x^n e^x-nI_{n-1}.$$ 
This formula is not the final answer by itself, but it explains the repeated reduction mechanism.

Now work a full example: evaluate $\int x^2 e^x\,dx$ using successive integration by parts.

Let
$$I_2=\int x^2 e^x\,dx.$$ 
First step:
$$I_2=x^2 e^x-\int 2x e^x\,dx \quad \text{(integration by parts)}$$
$$=x^2 e^x-2\int x e^x\,dx \quad \text{(factor out 2)}.$$ 
Now evaluate $\int x e^x\,dx$:
$$\int x e^x\,dx=x e^x-\int e^x\,dx \quad \text{(integration by parts)}$$
$$=x e^x-e^x+C.$$ 
Substitute back:
$$I_2=x^2 e^x-2(x e^x-e^x)+C \quad \text{(substitute the inner result)}$$
$$=x^2 e^x-2x e^x+2e^x+C$$
$$=e^x(x^2-2x+2)+C.$$ 
The same reduction logic works for $\int x^n\sin x\,dx$, though the signs alternate differently and a second application often introduces a paired integral with $\cos x$.

For logarithms, the idea is different: since $\ln x$ becomes simpler when differentiated, we choose $u=\ln x$ and $dv=dx$. This is one of the most important examples of strategic choice. Evaluate $\int \ln x\,dx$.

Choose
$$u=\ln x, \qquad dv=dx.$$ 
Then
$$du=\frac{1}{x}\,dx, \qquad v=x.$$ 
Apply the formula:
$$\int \ln x\,dx= x\ln x-\int x\cdot \frac{1}{x}\,dx \quad \text{(substitute $u,v,du$)}$$
$$=x\ln x-\int 1\,dx \quad \text{(simplify)}$$
$$=x\ln x-x+C.$$ 
This example illustrates why we do not wait for a product to contain an obvious polynomial before using integration by parts. A lone logarithm can be treated as $\ln x\cdot 1$.

A similar pattern appears in $\int x^n\ln x\,dx$. Choosing $u=\ln x$ and $dv=x^n\,dx$ makes the logarithm disappear at the cost of a simple polynomial antiderivative. For example, with $n=2$:
$$\int x^2\ln x\,dx$$
$$u=\ln x,\quad dv=x^2\,dx$$
$$du=\frac{1}{x}\,dx,\quad v=\frac{x^3}{3}$$
$$\int x^2\ln x\,dx=\frac{x^3}{3}\ln x-\int \frac{x^3}{3}\cdot \frac{1}{x}\,dx$$
$$=\frac{x^3}{3}\ln x-\frac{1}{3}\int x^2\,dx$$
$$=\frac{x^3}{3}\ln x-\frac{1}{3}\cdot \frac{x^3}{3}+C$$
$$=\frac{x^3}{3}\ln x-\frac{x^3}{9}+C.$$ 
This is the pattern to remember: logarithms are typically best chosen as $u$ because differentiation simplifies them.
keyPoints: Repeated integration by parts reduces $x^n e^x$ and $x^n\sin x$ to lower-degree cases.
For logarithmic integrals, choose $u=\ln x$ whenever possible.
The recurrence $I_n=\int x^n e^x\,dx=x^n e^x-nI_{n-1}$ captures the reduction pattern.
A lone $\ln x$ can be treated as $\ln x\cdot 1$ to use integration by parts.

sections.title: 4. Trigonometric Integrals: Powers of Sine and Cosine
sections.content:
Trigonometric integrals often look complicated because of high powers, but identities convert them into simpler expressions. The most important observation is parity: if one power is odd, we can usually save one factor and use $\sin^2 x=1-\cos^2 x$ or $\cos^2 x=1-\sin^2 x$ to rewrite the rest. If both powers are even, we typically use power-reduction formulas.

Consider $\int \sin^3 x\cos^2 x\,dx$. Since the sine power is odd, save one factor of $\sin x$ and convert the remaining $\sin^2 x$ into $1-\cos^2 x$. Here is the full calculation.

Start with
$$\int \sin^3 x\cos^2 x\,dx$$
$$=\int (\sin^2 x)(\sin x)\cos^2 x\,dx \quad \text{(separate one sine factor)}$$
$$=\int (1-\cos^2 x)\sin x\cos^2 x\,dx \quad \text{(use $\sin^2 x=1-\cos^2 x$)}.$$ 
Now let
$$u=\cos x, \qquad du=-\sin x\,dx.$$ 
Then
$$\int (1-\cos^2 x)\sin x\cos^2 x\,dx$$
$$=-\int (1-u^2)u^2\,du \quad \text{(substitution)}$$
$$=-\int (u^2-u^4)\,du \quad \text{(expand)}$$
$$=-\left(\frac{u^3}{3}-\frac{u^5}{5}\right)+C \quad \text{(integrate termwise)}$$
$$=-\frac{u^3}{3}+\frac{u^5}{5}+C \quad \text{(simplify)}$$
$$=-\frac{\cos^3 x}{3}+\frac{\cos^5 x}{5}+C.$$ 
So $\int \sin^3 x\cos^2 x\,dx=-\frac{\cos^3 x}{3}+\frac{\cos^5 x}{5}+C$.

Now consider a case where both powers are even: $\int \sin^2 x\cos^2 x\,dx$. Here no factor is odd, so parity alone does not immediately reduce the integral. Instead, use the identity
$$\sin^2 x\cos^2 x=\left(\frac{1}{2}\sin 2x\right)^2=\frac{1}{4}\sin^2 2x.$$ 
Then use the power-reduction identity $\sin^2 \theta=\frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}$:
$$\int \sin^2 x\cos^2 x\,dx$$
$$=\frac{1}{4}\int \sin^2 2x\,dx \quad \text{(rewrite with a double angle)}$$
$$=\frac{1}{4}\int \frac{1-\cos 4x}{2}\,dx \quad \text{(power reduction)}$$
$$=\frac{1}{8}\int (1-\cos 4x)\,dx$$
$$=\frac{1}{8}\left(x-\frac{\sin 4x}{4}\right)+C$$
$$=\frac{x}{8}-\frac{\sin 4x}{32}+C.$$ 
This illustrates the general rule: when powers are even, reduce them with identities until the integral becomes elementary.

Another useful trigonometric integral is $\int \sin^m x\cos^n x\,dx$. The method depends on the parity of $m$ and $n$. If one exponent is odd, use that factor to make a substitution. If both are even, reduce them using half-angle identities. The choice is guided by whether the identity turns the integrand into a polynomial in $\sin x$ or $\cos x$, or into a linear combination of cosines with multiple angles.

The key is not to rush to compute; first inspect the exponents. The structure determines the method.
keyPoints: If one trig power is odd, save one factor and use a Pythagorean identity.
If both trig powers are even, use power-reduction or double-angle formulas.
Rewrite trig products in a way that creates a substitution variable like $u=\sin x$ or $u=\cos x$.
Always inspect parity before expanding or differentiating anything.

sections.title: 5. Products of Sines and Cosines with Different Frequencies; Cyclic Integrals
sections.content:
Integrals of products like $\sin ax\cos bx$ are often handled by product-to-sum identities. These identities convert products into sums, and sums are usually simpler to integrate term by term. For example,
$$\sin A\cos B=\frac{1}{2}\bigl(\sin(A+B)+\sin(A-B)\bigr).$$ 
This is especially useful when the frequencies differ.

Work a complete example: evaluate $\int \sin 3x\cos 2x\,dx$.

Use the product-to-sum identity:
$$\sin 3x\cos 2x=\frac{1}{2}\bigl(\sin(5x)+\sin(x)\bigr) \quad \text{(apply the identity with $A=3x$, $B=2x$)}.$$ 
Then
$$\int \sin 3x\cos 2x\,dx$$
$$=\frac{1}{2}\int \bigl(\sin 5x+\sin x\bigr)\,dx \quad \text{(rewrite the product)}$$
$$=\frac{1}{2}\left(-\frac{\cos 5x}{5}-\cos x\right)+C \quad \text{(integrate termwise)}$$
$$=-\frac{\cos 5x}{10}-\frac{\cos x}{2}+C.$$ 
So the product-to-sum identity turns the problem into a routine integration.

Another common phenomenon is a cyclic integral, where integration by parts brings back the original integral. A classic example is $\int e^x\sin x\,dx$ or $\int e^x\cos x\,dx$. The trick is to apply integration by parts twice, producing an equation that can be solved algebraically.

Let us evaluate $I=\int e^x\sin x\,dx$ completely.

First integration by parts with
$$u=\sin x, \qquad dv=e^x\,dx$$
gives
$$du=\cos x\,dx, \qquad v=e^x.$$ 
So
$$I=e^x\sin x-\int e^x\cos x\,dx.$$ 
Let
$$J=\int e^x\cos x\,dx.$$ 
Now compute $J$ by parts:
$$u=\cos x, \qquad dv=e^x\,dx$$
$$du=-\sin x\,dx, \qquad v=e^x.$$ 
Then
$$J=e^x\cos x-\int e^x(-\sin x)\,dx$$
$$=e^x\cos x+I.$$ 
Substitute this back into the expression for $I$:
$$I=e^x\sin x-J$$
$$=e^x\sin x-(e^x\cos x+I) \quad \text{(replace $J$ with its formula)}$$
$$=e^x\sin x-e^x\cos x-I.$$ 
Now solve for $I$:
$$2I=e^x(\sin x-\cos x)$$
$$I=\frac{e^x}{2}(\sin x-\cos x)+C.$$ 
This is the cyclic integration pattern: after a finite number of steps, the original integral reappears, and algebra finishes the problem.

A similar method works for integrals such as $\int e^{ax}\sin bx\,dx$ and $\int e^{ax}\cos bx\,dx$. The repeated-by-parts equations can be solved by substitution or by algebraic completion. The important habit is to recognize when the integral is cyclic rather than trying to continue indefinitely.

One final warning: cyclic integrals are not a sign of failure. They are a signal to organize the algebra carefully. Once the original integral reappears, move all copies to one side and solve exactly as you would a linear equation.
keyPoints: Product-to-sum identities turn trig products into sums that integrate easily.
When a product integral reappears after integration by parts, isolate it algebraically.
Cyclic integrals are common for $\int e^{ax}\sin bx\,dx$ and $\int e^{ax}\cos bx\,dx$.
The goal is to recognize structure early and choose the identity that simplifies the integrand.

exercises.question: Derive the integration by parts formula starting from the product rule, and use it to evaluate $\int x e^x\,dx$.
exercises.solution:
Start with the product rule:
$$\frac{d}{dx}(uv)=u\frac{dv}{dx}+v\frac{du}{dx}.$$ 
Integrate both sides with respect to $x$:
$$uv=\int u\,dv+\int v\,du.$$ 
Rearrange to get the integration by parts formula:
$$\int u\,dv=uv-\int v\,du.$$ 
Now evaluate $\int x e^x\,dx$ by choosing
$$u=x,\qquad dv=e^x\,dx.$$ 
Then
$$du=dx,\qquad v=e^x.$$ 
Apply the formula:
$$\int x e^x\,dx=xe^x-\int e^x\,dx=xe^x-e^x+C.$$ 
So the integral is
$$\boxed{e^x(x-1)+C}.$$

exercises.question: Which choice of $u$ and $dv$ is best for evaluating $\int \ln x\,dx$ using integration by parts? Then compute the integral.
exercises.solution:
A good strategy is to choose the logarithm as $u$, because it simplifies when differentiated. Write
$$\int \ln x\,dx=\int \ln x\cdot 1\,dx.$$ 
So choose
$$u=\ln x,\qquad dv=dx.$$ 
Then
$$du=\frac{1}{x}\,dx,\qquad v=x.$$ 
Apply integration by parts:
$$\int \ln x\,dx=x\ln x-\int x\cdot \frac{1}{x}\,dx=x\ln x-\int 1\,dx.$$
Therefore,
$$\int \ln x\,dx=x\ln x-x+C.$$ 
So the best choice is $u=\ln x$ and $dv=dx$, and the value is
$$\boxed{x\ln x-x+C}.$$

exercises.question: Evaluate $\int x^2\sin x\,dx$ using repeated integration by parts.
exercises.solution:
Let
$$I=\int x^2\sin x\,dx.$$ 
Use integration by parts with
$$u=x^2,\qquad dv=\sin x\,dx.$$ 
Then
$$du=2x\,dx,\qquad v=-\cos x.$$ 
So
$$I=-x^2\cos x+\int 2x\cos x\,dx.$$ 
Now evaluate the remaining integral by parts again. Let
$$J=\int 2x\cos x\,dx.$$ 
Choose
$$u=2x,\qquad dv=\cos x\,dx,$$
so
$$du=2\,dx,\qquad v=\sin x.$$ 
Then
$$J=2x\sin x-\int 2\sin x\,dx=2x\sin x+2\cos x.$$ 
Substitute back:
$$I=-x^2\cos x+2x\sin x+2\cos x+C.$$ 
Thus
$$\boxed{\int x^2\sin x\,dx=-x^2\cos x+2x\sin x+2\cos x+C}.$$

exercises.question: Evaluate $\int \sin^3 x\cos^4 x\,dx$ by using trig identities and a substitution. Explain why your method works.
exercises.solution:
First inspect the parity of the powers. The sine power is odd: $\sin^3 x$. When one trig power is odd, save one factor and convert the rest using $\sin^2 x=1-\cos^2 x$.

Rewrite:
$$\sin^3 x\cos^4 x=\sin x\,\sin^2 x\cos^4 x=\sin x\,(1-\cos^2 x)\cos^4 x.$$ 
Now let
$$u=\cos x,\qquad du=-\sin x\,dx.$$ 
Then
$$\int \sin^3 x\cos^4 x\,dx=-\int (1-u^2)u^4\,du=-\int (u^4-u^6)\,du.$$ 
Integrate term by term:
$$-\left(\frac{u^5}{5}-\frac{u^7}{7}\right)+C=-\frac{u^5}{5}+\frac{u^7}{7}+C.$$ 
Substitute back $u=\cos x$:
$$\boxed{\int \sin^3 x\cos^4 x\,dx=-\frac{\cos^5 x}{5}+\frac{\cos^7 x}{7}+C}.$$ 
This works because the odd power lets us save one sine factor and use the Pythagorean identity to convert everything else into a polynomial in $\cos x$.

exercises.question: Evaluate the cyclic integral $\int e^{2x}\cos 3x\,dx$ using integration by parts and algebraic isolation.
exercises.solution:
Let
$$I=\int e^{2x}\cos 3x\,dx.$$ 
Use integration by parts with
$$u=\cos 3x,\qquad dv=e^{2x}\,dx.$$ 
Then
$$du=-3\sin 3x\,dx,\qquad v=\frac{1}{2}e^{2x}.$$ 
So
$$I=\frac{1}{2}e^{2x}\cos 3x+\frac{3}{2}\int e^{2x}\sin 3x\,dx.$$ 
Now define
$$J=\int e^{2x}\sin 3x\,dx.$$ 
Integrate by parts again with
$$u=\sin 3x,\qquad dv=e^{2x}\,dx,$$
so
$$du=3\cos 3x\,dx,\qquad v=\frac{1}{2}e^{2x}.$$ 
Then
$$J=\frac{1}{2}e^{2x}\sin 3x-\frac{3}{2}\int e^{2x}\cos 3x\,dx=\frac{1}{2}e^{2x}\sin 3x-\frac{3}{2}I.$$ 
Substitute this into the equation for $I$:
$$I=\frac{1}{2}e^{2x}\cos 3x+\frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}e^{2x}\sin 3x-\frac{3}{2}I\right).$$ 
Simplify:
$$I=\frac{1}{2}e^{2x}\cos 3x+\frac{3}{4}e^{2x}\sin 3x-\frac{9}{4}I.$$ 
Bring the $I$ terms together:
$$\frac{13}{4}I=e^{2x}\left(\frac{1}{2}\cos 3x+\frac{3}{4}\sin 3x\right).$$ 
Thus
$$I=\frac{e^{2x}}{13}\left(2\cos 3x+3\sin 3x\right)+C.$$ 
So
$$\boxed{\int e^{2x}\cos 3x\,dx=\frac{e^{2x}}{13}\left(2\cos 3x+3\sin 3x\right)+C}.$$

exercises.question: Suppose you encounter $\int x^3\ln x\,dx$ and $\int \sin^4 x\cos^3 x\,dx$. For each integral, explain the key structural feature you would inspect first and justify the strategy you would choose before calculating.
exercises.solution:
For $\int x^3\ln x\,dx$, the key structural feature is the presence of a logarithm multiplied by a polynomial. A logarithm is a strong candidate for $u$ in integration by parts because differentiating it simplifies it to $1/x$. The polynomial should go into $dv$ only if it is easy to integrate, which it is. So the natural strategy is
$$u=\ln x,\qquad dv=x^3\,dx.$$ 
This choice reduces the logarithm and leaves a simpler polynomial integral after one application of integration by parts.

For $\int \sin^4 x\cos^3 x\,dx$, the first thing to inspect is parity of the trig powers. Here the cosine power is odd. That suggests saving one factor of $\cos x$ and converting the rest using $\cos^2 x=1-\sin^2 x$, then substituting $u=\sin x$. Concretely,
$$\cos^3 x=\cos^2 x\cos x=(1-\sin^2 x)\cos x.$$ 
Then the whole integrand becomes a polynomial in $\sin x$ times $\cos x\,dx$, which is ideal for substitution. 

So the strategy is:
- For the first integral, choose the logarithm as $u$ because it simplifies under differentiation.
- For the second integral, inspect parity first and use the odd trig factor to create a substitution.

In both cases, the right move comes from recognizing what structure becomes simpler after differentiation or substitution rather than expanding immediately.

content.summary: This chapter developed integration by parts from the product rule and showed how the method turns difficult products into simpler integrals when $u$ and $dv$ are chosen strategically. We then extended the same reduction mindset to trigonometric integrals, using parity, identities, product-to-sum formulas, and cyclic algebra to manage powers and products of sines and cosines. The unifying theme is structure: identify what simplifies under differentiation, what is easy to integrate, and what identities transform an integral into a form you already know how to finish.
content.nextSteps: Next, practice mixed problems until the choice of method becomes automatic: integration by parts, tabular integration, trig identities, or cyclic algebra. In the following chapter, these tools will be combined with substitution and inverse trigonometric forms to expand your integration toolkit further.
